sentences.text: Well, there almost, almost a hundred people.
They were the leaders of the community and their wives.
And it was a very major event.

And my presentation or my [NON-ENGLISH] was taken from a topic which was introduced by my great-grandfather after whom I am named, [NON-ENGLISH] Yitzchak, or in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH] Shmelkes, who was the Orthodox rabbi of Lwow at the turn of the century.
No.
My father had some friends at the university because he was interested in Greek and Latin literature and classics.
So he had some friends.
Also, he had many contacts there with the non-Jewish community.
He was very highly regarded.
But it was not common to have too many relations with non-Jews.

The only contact which he had was with Archbishop Andrew Graf Szeptycki [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because of circumstances which existed in Lwow, namely, there was a very prominent man who celebrated his 70th birthday.
And when my father read that the Roman Catholic clergy ignored it, although he was a Roman Catholic archbishop-- Greek Catholic archbishop, not Roman Catholic.

So my father headed a delegation to greet the man in the name of the Jewish community because he was very friendly, very correct, toward the Jewish community and usually also made contributions to charitable causes.
There's evidence of it.
In your home-- we're talking about your bar mitzvah in 1938.
Yes.
Oh, yes, no question about it.
That's a very complicated question because, you see, people didn't have the knowledge which they have today.
Secondly, they didn't fully comprehend the depth of the hatred which was cultivated by the leaders of the Third Reich-- and the danger.
The danger came from two areas-- first of all, from the Red Terror, which was unfolding in USSR, and Hitler's activity-- and his government's activities in Germany.
But I was aware of it because it was discussed at home.
And the newspapers brought news of expulsion of Jews from Germany to Poland.
Namely, very many Jews of Polish origin emigrated to Germany in the '20s, lived in variety of cities.
And they had to leave.
And they were kept in a place, [NON-ENGLISH]-- actually, not far from Katowice-- for months before they were released into Poland.
So all this was permeating my life as a teenager.
No.
Indirectly-- no.
I have little-- I lived in sort of a cocoon of a teenager going to school.
However, the discussion whether to leave was always there.
My father was a Zionist, one of the few Orthodox rabbis who openly accepted and supported Zionism.
He was actually a member of the executive committee in Southern Poland.
He was the president of Keren Kayemeth.
He was the president of Keren Hayesod.
He politically had no affiliation with any specific group.
But he supported the immigration to what was then Eretz Yisrael or Palestine because this offered a channel of escape.
Therefore, he was highly critical of the Hasidic movement and the Orthodoxy, which tried to prevent that.
Because he saw Poland as a trap.
No, I was not interested.
And I kept aloof of any political activity.
Reason being is that I could always embarrass my father.
So I just kept aloof.
Horses, girls.
In park near our house.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was interested in sports and to squeeze through from grade to grade.
There was a shame not to be able to move from grade to grade, to be left behind.
Yes, I read an enormous amount.
History.
Yeah.
History, literature.
Oh yes, but I don't think we had a clear view.
Or for that matter, the pressures of studying were such that there was little time left for too much leisure.
And the introduction of current affairs into schools, it's a product of the United States at a much later date.
Yes.
Primarily to Eretz Yisrael, Palestine.
And one or two were sent to schools in England.
Well, we were at my grandfather's estate for a vacation, where we usually were.
And--
It was near Rawa Ruska.
And sometime around August the 20th, which was two weeks before we usually used to return to the city, my father, who was in Lwow, sent a cable saying that we should immediately return to the city.
So we cut our vacation short.
And my mother and her-- the three children-- myself and my two brothers-- returned by rail, as we usually did, to Lwow.
And you could sense the excitement and tension.
This was after the Germans have dismembered Czechoslovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And eventually, there was a mobilization.
No, because on September 1, they bombed the city, which was no more, no less but act of terror.
Because there were no military objectives of any kind in Lwow.
And secondly, there were no military concentration.
It was just an old city, just like bombing Florence-- exactly the same.
But this barbarism was an opening of a variety of things which happened subsequently.
And then the Germans approached Lwow after a few days.
And there was a siege.
But the Polish garrison defended itself, and rather successfully, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the Red Army crossed the eastern border of Poland.
And then it was unclear what will happen next.
Yes.
My mother's name was Rachel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Family name or maiden name was Reiss-- R-E-I-S-S.
Nathan was my younger brother.
And Leon was my youngest brother.

Oh, at once, because-- I don't remember the exact date, but my impression is that sometime in the third week of September, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Polish radio, radio station was still active and providing news.
I still remember distinctly playing Chopin music all day long.
And then it was cut off like with a knife.
And within hours, Soviet tanks appeared and Soviet soldiers.
When the city was occupied by the Red Army, which was very orderly, they didn't interfere with the local population at all in the first few days.
They jumped only at the stores, because the stores were functioning normally, and emptied the shelves.
And that's it.
First of all, food-- the distribution of food disintegrated.
So there was literally hunger.
Secondly, it took some time before water was-- the flow of water was restored.
And then winter came.
It was one of the most severe winters in my memory.
And we didn't have fuel.
So it was very cold.
Epidemics developed in the city, a variety of diseases, the--
Yes, I almost died of diphtheria because there was no serum.
And it was found with a great deal of difficulty.
And then this population of the city swelled because of refugees who came from Western Poland.
So life, in short, was quite miserable in the first year.

Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how to approach it because if you measure it by what we know today and by yardsticks of modern life, I think he perhaps was foolhardy because he resumed his position and his activities as a rabbi in the temple just as if nothing before.
And the temple was packed every Friday and Saturday.
He continued to preach.
And regardless of Stalin's terror and activities of the Russians in the city, he carried on.
Oh, yes, in a variety of ways.
First of all--
Personally, I was persecuted in the school because I was a son of a rabbi-- or as they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Clergyman.
So the new management of the school, which came from the Ukraine-- among them also some Jews-- were haunting me because I was a counter-revolutionary.
Pardon me?
There were many ways, you see.
There was the atmosphere of paranoia.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's well described.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was much more severe that-- what we know about conditions in the Soviet Union, which were described after the war until even Brezhnev's times.
This was midst of Stalin's terror.
And it was terror.
I was 14 in '39.
My father could be arrested any moment.
I personally could be arrested in the school for some counter-revolutionary activities, which was very strange.

And secondly, I was pointed out by this consort, which was organizer, which-- 'organizer of the Young Communists,' as an example of somebody who disrupts the school and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] causes sabotage.
Now, these were very dangerous things in those days.
Because by haunting me, she could provoke the arrest of my parents and deportation.
No.
I couldn't because of my social background.
But as I was reasonably resourceful, I became an instructor of the Red Cross-- first aid because it was non-political.
And as a senior instructor, eventually, of the Red Cross, I was meshed into the Civil Defense System, which was organized by the Russian military.
So I became, essentially, part of the mechanism and out of the clutches of the consort.
Go to school in the [NON-ENGLISH], 10 year program.
That's the name of the school.
There was-- previously a Jewish school, so most of the kids were Jewish.
It was gymnasium, which was transformed into a Soviet school.
I spent about until 2 o'clock or 3 o'clock at school.
And from then on, I had to go to work in my activities as an instructor of the Red Cross.
Yes.
Other kids-- this time, boys and girls because the Soviets have commingled a Jewish gymnasium for girls with boys and divided the students so that the classes were coeducation.
Oh, we were quite well aware of what was going on in the rest of Poland.
We knew that there's a persecution of Jews.
But we were not aware of the brutality or anything which would lead us to think that there's an extermination program.
We were certainly oppressed by the Soviets, but not to the same extent.
There was a semblance of normal life.
And after the first year under Soviet occupation, the supply situation has improved somewhat.
But-- and life was more normal.
But we were annexed to the Soviet Union.
So Lwow became a Soviet administrators' center.
And, on the other hand, this was the start of major arrests and deportations.
Well, I wasn't interested in Hebrew studies before the war.
So I didn't go out of my way to resume it under the Soviet occupation.
I just wasn't too interested in it.
Well, surely I remember that.
First of all, the city was bombed again by the Germans.
And there was heavy fighting-- not in the city but in the surrounding area-- for almost a week.
And then the Russians started to withdraw and evacuate.
There were very many casualties.
And as I was in first-aid unit-- so I was going to the places which were bombed out to remove the bodies and help the wounded.
It was considerable chaos.
And we, again, lived in the cellar, as we did in '39-- hunger, lack of water, and the usual.
The Germans arrived.

The first day, nothing changed except that the city was decorated with blue and yellow flags because the Ukrainians had the illusion that a Ukraine would be created-- independent Ukraine-- by the Germans.
And the second day was still quiet.
But then the pogrom started.
Hm?
That's impossible to answer because--

I was-- my father went to the Metropolitan to Archbishop Szeptycki, a highly influential man, and was-- actually prepared a few comments in Polish, which I translated for him in Ukrainian which I had very rudimentary knowledge.
And before going there, he-- his barber came to give him a haircut, which was normal at that time.
He put on rabbinical clothes and went there in rabbinical garb, accompanied by two senior members of the community.
And I said goodbye to my father.
And I was lying on the couch and reading a book.
Because there were rumors that the Ukrainian population is attacking Jews.
And consequently, he went to his friend asking for intervention.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after my father left, I just waited for him to come back.
And I was lying on the couch, reading a book, engrossed.
I still remember the book.
It was one of these western novels written in the United States in English translated into Polish.
Suddenly, four or five young Ukrainians came in and dragged me out into the street, where I was beaten and dragged into the prison.
And I didn't know what-- before I knew what was happening to me, I found myself in the city prison, which was not too far-- about a block and a half away.
My mother was home.
But they didn't touch women at the time, or children-- just men.
I was 16 years old.
So I was considered already a man.
Not only in the prison, but first on the street, because they dragged other men.
So they were all chasing out and beating us, you see, with rifles and all kinds of sticks and fists, and chasing us through the center of the street, not on the sidewalks.
On the sidewalks, you have Poles and Ukrainians clapping hands and saying-- well, enjoying this, this spectacle.
And this whole group was run into the prison.
That's it.
In the prison, we were chased into a corner and put to work to drag out bodies of prisoners who were executed by the NKVD.
And what they did is they sealed them in cellars and set the prison on fire.
There must be a few thousand bodies there.
They were doing it probably from early spring, sometimes-- yes, they must have started it sometime, in my opinion, around March, April.
Yes.
No, they first executed the people and threw the bodies into the cellars and sealed the cellars.

Well, that is a very difficult question because the criteria were Polish officers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] policemen, owners of large shops, bankers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] newspapermen, politicians, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people in public eye engaged in some political activity, government officials.
They had their criteria.
And the criteria of the Soviet regime was very difficult to fathom.
Not really.
No.
No.
We heard shooting, but we didn't know what it is.
Oh yes, there were very many members of the Jewish community-- bankers, newspapermen, Zionists, politicians, anything.
And my father was probably a candidate.
They hadn't bothered him because he was too influential and would cause too much of a commotion.
But eventually, that would be the case, too.
We were--
--brought there as Jews.
And the Ukrainian nationalists were screaming and howling that we are the murderers.
We are communists because we are Jews.
We killed Christ-- the usual.
And we were given the ropes.
And some people had to go down.
And the bodies were brought out.
And I was working there.
While I was working there, I've seen my father marched in with other Jews into a corner.
But after the first group was brought in, from then on, they collected people in a corner of the huge yard, and put two machine guns, and executed them, along with my father.
End of story.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Brygidki.
Yes.
[LAUGHS] It depends where.
[LAUGHS] At first, it was probably a military sort of a campus [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or compound and must have been also a prison.
And they gave it a name of a saint-- Saint-- Sainte Birgitta, or something.
In colloquial language, it's called Brygidki--
Yes.
No.
Both.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
And there were two kinds--
There were three kinds of Germans.
One, you had their Germans belong-- or people in German uniform belonging to a Ukrainian battalion called Nachtigall.
Nachtigall was a group of saboteurs and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fifth Column organized in Western Poland occupied by the Germans in preparation for attacking the Soviet Union.
And they had German uniforms and blue and yellow ribbon-- patches.
They were commanded by future minister of refugees in the government of Konrad Adenauer, Dr.
Theodor Oberlander.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was one group of the henchmen.
The second one were German soldiers.
They probably were also some SS people, also German police-- Feldgendarmerie.
So it's very hard to know.
And besides, I was not inclined to [LAUGHS]--
--make any inquiries because people were shot, and quickly.
All day long, from roughly 10 o'clock in the morning.
And we were thrown out of there, the survivors, around probably 7 o'clock in the evening.
Back home.
Well, I would say probably a distance of one city block.
Well, I came back home.
Obviously, I smelled of, of corpses.
And that's the very characteristic Swedish smell.
So I washed up.
And I-- people were waiting at my house, my neighbors, because there are two of us who came out.
And we told them what happened.
They wouldn't believe.
And--
Myself, and a son-of-law of my neighbor, a friend of my parents who were much older.
And he was a pharmacist.
And he said to me, let's commit suicide, so we don't have to go there-- back tomorrow morning.
And my mother talked me out of it.
They had told us to come back.
But my mother said, you will not go.
And I was hiding in the apartment on some library shelves.
No.
No, no, no.
No.
